facts.text: Michigan Stadium, known as 'The Big House,' is the largest stadium in the United States with an official capacity of over 107,000.
The Heisman Trophy has been awarded annually since 1935 to the most outstanding player in college football.
The rivalry between Michigan and Ohio State, known simply as 'The Game,' is widely considered the greatest rivalry in American sports.
Alabama's Nick Saban won six national championships with the Crimson Tide, the most by any coach at a single school in the modern era.
The Rose Bowl, first played in 1902, is the oldest bowl game in college football and is nicknamed 'The Granddaddy of Them All.'
Notre Dame's 'Play Like a Champion Today' sign, which players tap on their way to the field, has been a tradition since the late 1980s.
The first college football game was played on November 6, 1869, between Rutgers and Princeton, with Rutgers winning 6–4.
Bear Bryant won 323 games as a head coach, including six national championships at Alabama.
The SEC has produced more NFL first-round draft picks than any other conference in college football history.
Ohio State's tradition of 'Dotting the i' in Script Ohio is one of the most iconic marching band performances in sports.
The Iron Bowl between Alabama and Auburn gets its name from the game historically being played in Birmingham, Alabama's iron and steel center.
Clemson players touch Howard's Rock at the top of the hill before running down into Death Valley, a tradition started in 1966.
Archie Griffin of Ohio State is the only player to win the Heisman Trophy twice, earning it in 1974 and 1975.
The College Football Playoff replaced the BCS system in 2014, initially featuring a four-team format.
Knute Rockne compiled a career record of 105–12–5 at Notre Dame, giving him the highest winning percentage in college football history.
Tiger Stadium at LSU is known as 'Death Valley' because opposing teams rarely leave with a win under the lights on Saturday night.
The Red River Rivalry between Texas and Oklahoma is played annually at the Cotton Bowl in Dallas during the State Fair of Texas.
USC's Trojans got their nickname in 1912 when a sportswriter compared the team's fighting spirit to the ancient warriors of Troy.
The Army-Navy Game has been played annually since 1890 and is one of the most tradition-rich rivalries in all of sports.
Oklahoma won 47 consecutive games from 1953 to 1957 under coach Bud Wilkinson, the longest winning streak in major college football history.
Bo Schembechler won or shared 13 Big Ten titles in 21 seasons at Michigan but never won a national championship.
The 'Kick Six' in the 2013 Iron Bowl saw Auburn's Chris Davis return a missed field goal 109 yards for a touchdown as time expired.
Neyland Stadium in Knoxville, Tennessee, has a capacity of over 100,000, making it one of the largest stadiums in the world.
The first Heisman Trophy was awarded to Jay Berwanger of the University of Chicago in 1935.
Georgia's mascot Uga is a live English Bulldog that has attended games since 1956, with each successive dog inheriting the name.
Bobby Bowden won two national championships at Florida State and accumulated 377 career victories across all programs.
The term 'bowl game' originates from the Rose Bowl Stadium, which was bowl-shaped, and other postseason games adopted the naming convention.
Penn State's 'White Out' games, where the entire stadium wears white, create one of the most intimidating atmospheres in college football.
In 1984, Doug Flutie threw a game-winning Hail Mary pass for Boston College against Miami, one of the most famous plays in college football history.
Texas A&M's 12th Man tradition began in 1922 when a student suited up and stood ready to play after injuries depleted the roster.
The Sugar Bowl has been played in New Orleans since 1935 and is one of the original 'New Year's Six' bowl games.
Woody Hayes coached Ohio State for 28 seasons, winning five national championships and 13 Big Ten titles.
The Florida Gators' 'Gator Chomp' is one of the most recognizable fan celebrations in college football.
Boise State's Statue of Liberty play to win the 2007 Fiesta Bowl against Oklahoma is considered one of the greatest plays in bowl game history.
The Pac-12 Conference, originally the Pacific Coast Conference, was founded in 1915 and underwent major realignment in the 2020s.
Joe Paterno won 409 games at Penn State, though some victories were later vacated due to NCAA sanctions.
Michigan's winged helmet design, introduced by Fritz Crisler in 1938, is one of the most iconic in all of sports.
The Orange Bowl has been played in the Miami area since 1935 and has hosted numerous national championship games.
Urban Meyer went 187–32 as a head coach, winning three national championships at Florida and Ohio State.
LSU's tradition of playing 'Neck' by Cameo after big plays was famously banned by the university but still chanted by fans.
The Cotton Bowl Classic was first played in 1937 and was long associated with the Southwest Conference champion.
The Yale Bulldogs have won the most national championships in college football history, though most came in the sport's earliest era.
Harvard and Yale's rivalry, known as 'The Game,' has been played since 1875, making it one of the oldest in college football.
Alabama's Crimson Tide nickname reportedly originated from a 1907 newspaper account describing the team's muddy red jerseys.
Dabo Swinney led Clemson to two national championships in 2016 and 2018, transforming the program into a powerhouse.
The Big Ten Conference, despite its name, has had varying numbers of member schools throughout its history, currently exceeding 14.
Oklahoma's Sooner Schooner, a Conestoga wagon pulled by ponies, charges onto the field after every Sooner score.
The 1971 'Game of the Century' between Nebraska and Oklahoma drew massive national attention, with Nebraska winning 35–31.
Florida State's Chief Osceola plants a flaming spear at midfield before every home game, a tradition since 1978.
The 2006 Rose Bowl between Texas and USC is widely regarded as one of the greatest college football games ever played.
Kirby Smart won the national championship at Georgia in 2021, ending a 41-year drought for the Bulldogs.
Notre Dame remains the only major program to maintain its independence from a football conference as of the early 2020s.
The Heisman Trophy is named after John Heisman, a pioneering coach who helped establish the forward pass as a fundamental part of the game.
Tennessee's tradition of running through the 'T' formed by the Pride of the Southland Marching Band has been a pregame staple since 1965.
Cal's 'The Play' in 1982 featured five laterals on the kickoff return, with the final ball carrier plowing through Stanford's band in the end zone.
The Big 12 Conference was formed in 1996 from a merger of the Big Eight and four Southwest Conference schools.
USC has produced more NFL first-round draft picks than any other school in college football history.
The Forward Pass was legalized in 1906 to reduce the brutality of the game after 18 players died during the 1905 season.
Wisconsin's 'Jump Around' tradition at Camp Randall Stadium, where fans jump to House of Pain's hit, registers on seismographs.
The ACC was founded in 1953 and has grown from its original seven members to become one of the largest conferences in college football.
Tim Tebow became the first sophomore to win the Heisman Trophy in 2007.
Ohio State's Buckeye nickname comes from the Ohio buckeye tree, and players earn buckeye leaf stickers on their helmets for big plays.
The 2018 College Football Playoff National Championship saw Alabama defeat Georgia 26–23 in overtime on a walk-off touchdown pass.
Auburn's Toomer's Corner, where fans roll the oak trees with toilet paper after victories, is one of college football's quirkiest traditions.
The first nationally televised college football game was the 1952 matchup between Notre Dame and Penn.
Clemson's Death Valley got its nickname because Presbyterian College coach Lonnie McMilllan said his team was going to 'Death Valley' before playing there.
The Peach Bowl, now the Chick-fil-A Peach Bowl, was first played in 1968 and became a major bowl in the College Football Playoff rotation.
Alabama has the most appearances in the AP Poll's final No. 1 ranking, reflecting its sustained dominance over decades.
The Michigan Wolverines have won the most games in college football history with over 1,000 victories.
Georgia's 'Between the Hedges' refers to the privet hedges that have surrounded Sanford Stadium's playing field since 1929.
The BCS (Bowl Championship Series) determined the national champion from 1998 to 2013 using a combination of polls and computer rankings.
The 1994 Penn State team went 12–0 but was denied a share of the national championship under the bowl coalition system, fueling calls for a playoff.
Texas's Bevo, a longhorn steer mascot, has attended Longhorn games since 1916 and is one of the most famous live mascots in sports.
Vince Young's game-winning touchdown run in the 2006 Rose Bowl capped a comeback against USC and is one of the most replayed plays in college football.
The first College Football Hall of Fame was established in 1951 and is currently located in Atlanta, Georgia.
Oklahoma has the most conference championships in college football history, largely accumulated during their Big Eight and Big 12 eras.
The 'War Eagle' battle cry of Auburn is separate from their Tigers nickname—Auburn is the only school with two such distinct traditions.
The Fiesta Bowl was first played in 1971 and rose to national prominence by hosting several memorable championship matchups.
Bear Bryant's houndstooth hat became so iconic that it is displayed in the Paul W. Bryant Museum at the University of Alabama.
Mississippi State's cowbells are so disruptive that the SEC banned artificial noisemakers, though an exception was later granted.
Stanford's mascot is the Cardinal (the color), not a cardinal bird, making it one of the most unusual team names in college sports.
The 1966 Notre Dame vs. Michigan State game, known as 'The Game of the Century,' ended in a controversial 10–10 tie.
The College Football Playoff expanded to 12 teams starting with the 2024–25 season, featuring first-round games on campus.
Ralphie the Buffalo charges across the field before every Colorado home game, guided by a team of handlers.
The 2013 BCS National Championship Game saw Alabama defeat Notre Dame 42–14 in a dominant performance.
Reggie Bush's 2005 Heisman Trophy was vacated due to NCAA violations, the first time that had happened in the award's history.
Nebraska's sellout streak of home games stretches back to 1962, one of the longest in all of sports.
Virginia Tech's 'Enter Sandman' entrance, where fans jump in unison to Metallica, creates a mini-earthquake effect at Lane Stadium.
The 2017 National Championship Game between Clemson and Alabama was decided by a last-second touchdown pass from Deshaun Watson.
The Southwest Conference, which included Texas, Texas A&M, and Arkansas, dissolved in 1996 due to financial and competitive imbalance.
Eddie George won the 1995 Heisman Trophy at Ohio State after rushing for 1,927 yards and 24 touchdowns.
The AP Poll has been ranking college football teams since 1936, making it the oldest continuous poll in the sport.
Clemson's Hill and Howard's Rock were added to Memorial Stadium in 1942, and the team has run down the hill before games ever since.
The Egg Bowl between Mississippi State and Ole Miss features the Golden Egg trophy, one of college football's most unique rivalry prizes.
In 2007, Appalachian State defeated No. 5 Michigan 34–32, widely considered the greatest upset in college football history.
Notre Dame's 'Touchdown Jesus' mural on the Hesburgh Library is visible from inside the stadium and has become an iconic symbol.
Jim Thorpe, one of the greatest athletes ever, played football at the Carlisle Indian Industrial School and won the 1912 Olympic decathlon.
The 'Bluegrass Miracle' in 2002 saw LSU score on a 74-yard Hail Mary to defeat Kentucky 33–30.
Texas's 'Hook 'em Horns' hand sign was created by student Harley Clark in 1955 and has been a Longhorn staple ever since.
The Rose Bowl Stadium in Pasadena, California, has hosted five Super Bowls in addition to its iconic college football bowl game.
Barry Sanders rushed for 2,628 yards in a single season at Oklahoma State in 1988, an NCAA record that still stands.
Georgia Tech's Ramblin' Wreck, a 1930 Ford Model A Sport Coupe, leads the team onto the field before every home game.
The first overtime game in college football under the current rules occurred in 1996 after the NCAA adopted the overtime format.
Nick Saban's coaching tree includes multiple head coaches across the NFL and college football, spreading his defensive philosophy nationwide.
Oregon's Phil Knight and Nike connection has given the Ducks some of the most creative and varied uniforms in college football.
The 2005 Rose Bowl between Texas and Michigan is remembered for Texas's 38–37 come-from-behind victory.
The concept of 'homecoming' originated in college football, with the University of Missouri claiming to have held the first homecoming in 1911.
Johnny Manziel became the first freshman to win the Heisman Trophy in 2012, earning the nickname 'Johnny Football.'
Peyton Manning, despite a stellar career at Tennessee, never won a national championship with the Volunteers.
The 'Immaculate Deflection' in the 2018 SEC Championship helped Georgia's Kirby Smart cement his program's rise.
Ohio State and Michigan have met every year since 1918, with only wartime interrupting the earlier years of the rivalry.
The Coaches Poll, run by the American Football Coaches Association, has been a fixture since 1950.
The 1979 Sugar Bowl featured No. 1 Penn State vs. No. 2 Alabama, with Alabama winning 14–7 for the national title.
Miami's 'Turnover Chain,' introduced in 2017, became one of the most imitated sideline celebrations in college football.
LSU's live tiger mascot Mike has been a tradition since 1936, and the tiger's habitat sits adjacent to Tiger Stadium.
The first night game in college football was played in 1892 between Wyoming Seminary and Mansfield State.
Troy Smith won the 2006 Heisman Trophy at Ohio State in one of the most lopsided votes in the award's history.
The Bayou Classic between Grambling State and Southern University is the premier HBCU football rivalry game.
Wisconsin's Camp Randall Stadium is built on the site of a Civil War training camp, making it one of the most historic venues in the sport.
The 2003 Fiesta Bowl saw Ohio State defeat Miami 31–24 in double overtime to win the BCS National Championship.
The 'Bush Push' in the 2005 USC–Notre Dame game saw Reggie Bush push Matt Leinart into the end zone for the winning score.
Florida's 'Mr. Two Bits' led the Gator crowd in a cheering tradition from 1949 to 2008.
The 1984 Orange Bowl saw Miami upset top-ranked Nebraska 31–30, launching the Hurricanes dynasty.
Lamar Jackson became Louisville's first Heisman Trophy winner in 2016 with a spectacular dual-threat season.
The 'Surrender Cobra,' the hands-on-head gesture of disbelief, became an internet sensation thanks to college football broadcasts.
College GameDay, ESPN's flagship Saturday morning show, has been broadcasting live from campuses since 1993.
The 'Miracle on the Plains' in 2013 saw Auburn's Nick Marshall throw a tipped pass that was caught for a 73-yard touchdown against Georgia.
The SEC has won more national championships in the 21st century than any other conference.
Army and Navy's football rivalry has featured the 'Commander-in-Chief's Trophy' since 1972, also including Air Force.
The 2002 Ohio State Buckeyes went 14–0 to win the national championship, the first team to play 14 games in a season.
Texas's 'Big Bertha' bass drum, introduced in 1955, was once the world's largest bass drum used by a university band.
Florida State went wire-to-wire at No. 1 in 1999, winning every game to capture the national title.
The Manning family—Archie, Peyton, and Eli—all played college football at Ole Miss or Tennessee, becoming football royalty.
The ACC added Notre Dame as a partial member in 2013, though the Fighting Irish retained football independence.
In 1982, Herschel Walker won the Heisman Trophy as a junior at Georgia and then left for the USFL.
The tradition of tailgating before college football games is deeply rooted in American culture and dates to the mid-19th century.
Mike Leach popularized the 'Air Raid' offense at Texas Tech and later Washington State, revolutionizing passing in college football.
The 2000 Orange Bowl featured Tom Brady's Michigan team losing to Alabama 35–34 in overtime.
The first forward pass in a college football game is often credited to Bradbury Robinson of Saint Louis University in 1906.
Death Valley, LSU's Tiger Stadium, once registered a seismic event during a game, caused by fans cheering a big play.
Deshaun Watson threw for 420 yards in the 2017 National Championship Game, leading Clemson to a 35–31 win over Alabama.
Ohio State's 'Buckeye Battle Cry' and 'Hang On Sloopy' are among the most beloved stadium anthems in the sport.
The wishbone offense, popularized by Texas and Oklahoma in the 1970s, transformed college football strategy.
Marcus Allen won the 1981 Heisman Trophy at USC after setting the NCAA single-season rushing record with 2,342 yards.
Baylor's rise under Art Briles saw the Bears win Big 12 titles in 2013 and 2014 after decades of mediocrity.
The Territorial Cup between Arizona and Arizona State is the oldest rivalry trophy in college football, dating to 1899.
Ricky Williams broke Tony Dorsett's career rushing record at Texas in 1998 and won the Heisman Trophy that season.
The 'Philly Special,' though famous from the NFL, was inspired by a play Clemson ran in a college game.
The Fighting Irish of Notre Dame get their nickname from the school's Irish-Catholic heritage and the fighting spirit of their early teams.
The Heisman Trophy weighs approximately 25 pounds and depicts a player in a stiff-arm pose.
The first black player to win the Heisman Trophy was Ernie Davis of Syracuse in 1961.
Clemson's tradition of releasing balloons after the first score of a home game was eventually discontinued for environmental reasons.
The 1945 Army-Navy game drew over 100,000 fans to Municipal Stadium in Philadelphia.
Florida won back-to-back national championships in 2006 and 2008 under Urban Meyer, with Tim Tebow starring in the second.
The Crimson Tide's elephant mascot, Big Al, has been a fixture of Alabama football since 1979.
The oldest college football stadium still in use is Franklin Field at Penn, opened in 1895.
The 'Goal Line Stand' in the 1979 Sugar Bowl, where Alabama stopped Penn State four times from the one-yard line, is legendary.
Kirk Herbstreit and Lee Corso have become the most recognized analysts in college football through their roles on ESPN's GameDay.
Nebraska's 1995 team, which went 12–0 and outscored opponents by an average of 38 points per game, is considered one of the greatest ever.
The 'Wide Right' series of missed Florida State field goals against Miami in the late 1980s and early 1990s defined the rivalry.
Colorado's 1990 national championship was controversial, as the Buffaloes were awarded a 'fifth down' in a key win over Missouri.
The first instant replay review in college football was used during the 2006 season.
Oklahoma's 1956 team under Bud Wilkinson outscored opponents 466–51 during a perfect 10–0 season.
The 'Bluebonnet Bowl,' once a major postseason game in Houston, was discontinued after the 1987 season.
The 2011 BCS National Championship saw Alabama and LSU play for a second time that season, a controversial rematch that Alabama won 21–0.
Cam Newton won the 2010 Heisman Trophy and led Auburn to a national championship in the same season.
Michigan's 'Go Blue' chant and The Victors fight song are considered among the most iconic in college football.
The concept of scholarship limits in college football was introduced to create competitive balance among programs.
In the 2007 season, No. 1 and No. 2 ranked teams lost on the same weekend a record-tying four times.
College football's overtime rules differ from the NFL, with each team getting a possession from the opponent's 25-yard line.
The 'Sooner Magic' at Oklahoma refers to the program's history of dramatic comebacks and last-minute victories.
Deion Sanders played both football and baseball at Florida State, excelling in both sports simultaneously.
Southern Cal's 'Traveler,' a white horse ridden by a Trojan warrior, has circled the Coliseum after touchdowns since 1961.
The 2009 Texas–Nebraska Big 12 Championship Game ended on a controversial replay review of a last-second field goal with one second added.
Penn State's all-white home uniforms with no names on the back reflect Joe Paterno's philosophy that the team is bigger than any individual.
Charles Woodson became the first primarily defensive player to win the Heisman Trophy in 1997 at Michigan.
The 'Gator Bowl' in Jacksonville, Florida, has been played since 1946 and was one of the early bowl games outside the Big Four.
Alabama's dynasty under Saban from 2009 to 2020 included six national titles and is unmatched in the modern era.
The 'Little Brown Jug' is the trophy awarded to the winner of the Michigan–Minnesota rivalry, dating to 1903.
The 1992 'Snow Bowl' between Notre Dame and Penn State was played in a blizzard, with Notre Dame winning 17–16.
Bo Jackson won the 1985 Heisman Trophy at Auburn and went on to become a two-sport professional star in football and baseball.
Florida State's Warchant, accompanied by the tomahawk chop, has been one of the most recognizable traditions in college football since the 1980s.
The 2023 National Championship game saw Georgia defeat TCU 65–7, one of the most lopsided title games ever.
The number of bowl games has grown from just four in the 1940s to over 40 in the modern era.
The 'Palmetto Bowl' between Clemson and South Carolina has been played since 1896 and is a fierce in-state rivalry.
Kyler Murray won the 2018 Heisman Trophy at Oklahoma and was later the No. 1 overall pick in the 2019 NFL Draft.
The 2015 Michigan State–Michigan game ended on a botched punt, with Michigan State's Jalen Watts-Jackson returning it for a touchdown on the last play.
The 'Cy-Hawk Trophy' is awarded to the winner of the Iowa–Iowa State rivalry game.
Ohio State's 2014 national championship team won the first-ever College Football Playoff with their third-string quarterback Cardale Jones.
Colorado's Ralphie V served as the live buffalo mascot from 2008 to 2019, continuing a tradition dating back to 1934.
The 1926 Rose Bowl featured Alabama dismantling Washington 20–19, putting Southern football on the national map.
Ara Parseghian rebuilt Notre Dame football in the 1960s, winning two consensus national titles during his tenure.
The 'Red River Showdown' between Texas and Oklahoma splits the Cotton Bowl stadium 50–50, with each side wearing their team's colors.
Desmond Howard's iconic Heisman pose after a punt return touchdown against Ohio State in 1991 became one of college football's defining images.
The 2016 Clemson–Alabama National Championship was decided 35–31, widely considered one of the best title games in CFP history.
Notre Dame's golden dome and the Word of Life mural (Touchdown Jesus) are among the most photographed landmarks in college football.
The transfer portal, introduced in 2018, fundamentally changed how players move between programs in college football.
USC's 2004 team, which went 13–0 and won the BCS title, later had its championship vacated due to NCAA violations.
The 'Horns Down' gesture by opposing fans toward Texas has become one of the more controversial taunts in Big 12 football.
Miami's five national championships between 1983 and 2001 established the Hurricanes as a modern dynasty.
The 1988 Notre Dame team went 12–0 and won the national championship under Lou Holtz, the last title for the Irish.
College football teams in the FBS are allowed 85 scholarship players, a limit established by the NCAA.
LSU's 2019 team, led by Joe Burrow, went 15–0 and is considered by many to be the greatest college football team of all time.
Joe Burrow won the 2019 Heisman Trophy in a historic landslide, receiving the highest percentage of first-place votes ever.
The 'Backyard Brawl' between Pittsburgh and West Virginia is one of the most intense rivalries in the eastern United States.
The SEC Championship Game has been played in Atlanta since 1992 and often has national title implications.
Texas Tech's 'Guns Up' hand sign was adopted in 1971 to represent the Red Raiders' dual-pistol logo.
The 1969 'Game of the Century' between Texas and Arkansas was attended by President Richard Nixon, who controversially declared Texas No. 1.
Pat Tillman played safety for Arizona State before joining the U.S. Army after the September 11 attacks, making the ultimate sacrifice.
The Navy Midshipmen have played their games at various venues, but Navy-Marine Corps Memorial Stadium in Annapolis has been their home since 1959.
The 2019 LSU–Alabama game, won by LSU 46–41, was one of the highest-scoring matchups in the rivalry's history.
Walk-on players who earn scholarships, known as 'scholarship walk-ons,' are a beloved tradition in college football programs.
The 1985 Chicago Bears' dominant defense was led by several former college football standouts, including Mike Singletary from Baylor.
The 'Horseshoe,' Ohio State's iconic stadium, opened in 1922 and has been expanded multiple times to its current capacity of over 100,000.
The 2008 national championship game saw Florida, led by Tim Tebow, dominate Oklahoma 24–14.
Danny Wuerffel won the 1996 Heisman Trophy at Florida while leading the Gators to a national title under Steve Spurrier.
The 'Iron Bowl' has been played at neutral sites and home stadiums throughout its history, adding to the rivalry's intrigue.
Oklahoma State's 'Paddle People' fans wave orange paddles in the stands, creating a unique visual atmosphere.
The 2022 College Football Playoff saw Georgia repeat as national champions with a 65–7 demolition of TCU.
The Sun Bowl in El Paso, Texas, is the second-oldest bowl game, first played in 1935.
Howard 'Hopalong' Cassady won the 1955 Heisman Trophy at Ohio State, earning his nickname for his elusive running style.
Georgia's Sanford Stadium features a natural hedgerow that gives the field its famous 'Between the Hedges' nickname.
College football's targeting rule, introduced in 2013, was designed to reduce dangerous helmet-to-helmet hits.
The 2014 inaugural College Football Playoff National Championship saw Ohio State defeat Oregon 42–20.
The 'Land Grant Trophy' between Michigan State and Penn State has been widely mocked as one of the ugliest rivalry trophies.
USC's 'Song Girls' are cheerleaders who have been a Trojans sideline tradition since 1967.
The annual spring game is a intrasquad scrimmage that gives fans their first look at the team before the fall season.
Charlie Ward won the 1993 Heisman Trophy at Florida State while also being a standout point guard for the basketball team.
The first college football game played under lights at night occurred in 1892, long before night games became common.
The Paul 'Bear' Bryant Award is given annually to the best coach in college football, named after the legendary Alabama coach.
Oregon's Autzen Stadium is known for its deafening noise despite having a capacity under 55,000 due to its enclosed design.
Washington's Husky Stadium sits on the shores of Lake Washington, and fans often arrive by boat on game days.
The 1987 Fiesta Bowl between Penn State and Miami, billed as a clash of styles, saw Penn State win the national championship 14–10.
The phrase 'student-athlete' was coined by the NCAA in the 1950s, and its meaning has been debated ever since.
Alabama's '4th and 31' conversion against Auburn in 2021 is remembered as one of the most improbable plays in rivalry history.
The 'Beamer Ball' philosophy at Virginia Tech under Frank Beamer emphasized special teams as a way to compete against more talented opponents.
The Citrus Bowl in Orlando, Florida, has been a New Year's Day staple since 1947 under various names.
Earl Campbell won the 1977 Heisman Trophy at Texas and became one of the most feared running backs in NFL history.
The 'Kickoff Classic' was a preseason game played from 1983 to 2002 that often featured top-ranked teams.
Michigan State's 'Sparty' statue, a bronze figure of a Spartan warrior, has been a campus landmark since 1945.
The 2007 Fiesta Bowl featured Boise State's hook-and-lateral and Statue of Liberty plays to upset Oklahoma in one of the greatest bowl games ever.
College football conferences receive revenue from television contracts worth billions of dollars, driving much of the sport's realignment.
Auburn's 'War Eagle' flight, where a golden eagle soars around Jordan-Hare Stadium before games, is one of the most dramatic entrances in sports.
The 'Victory Bell' is the rivalry trophy between UCLA and USC, painted in the winning team's colors each year.
The 'Palmetto State' rivalry between Clemson and South Carolina produces intense emotions throughout the state every November.
Roger Staubach won the 1963 Heisman Trophy as a junior at Navy before serving in the military and then starring in the NFL.
The 2010 'Cam-back' game saw Cam Newton rally Auburn from a 24–0 deficit to beat Alabama 28–27.
Tommy Tuberville's Auburn team went undefeated in 2004 but was shut out of the BCS National Championship Game, fueling playoff debates.
Manti Te'o's catfishing scandal at Notre Dame in 2013 became one of the biggest off-field stories in college football history.
The ACC's merger with several Pac-12 schools in the 2020s dramatically reshaped the conference landscape.
The Biletnikoff Award is given annually to the best wide receiver in college football, named after Oakland Raiders legend Fred Biletnikoff.
Steve Spurrier won the 1966 Heisman Trophy at Florida and later returned to coach the Gators to a national championship.
Texas's 2005 national championship team went 13–0 with Vince Young, Cedric Benson, and a roster loaded with future NFL players.
The College Football Playoff selection committee, made up of 13 members, has been both praised and criticized for its ranking decisions.
The 'Platoon System,' where different players play offense and defense, was popularized in the 1940s and changed the sport forever.
Georgia Tech's triple-option offense under Paul Johnson was one of the last remaining option-based schemes in major college football.
The 'Wally Pipp' story of college football involves numerous backup quarterbacks who stepped in and became stars.
The 2021 Alabama–Texas A&M game ended in a shocking 41–38 upset of the No. 1 Crimson Tide.
The Outland Trophy is awarded annually to the best interior lineman in college football.
In 2020, the Big Ten initially canceled the fall football season due to COVID-19 before reversing course and playing a shortened schedule.
SMU received the NCAA 'death penalty' in 1987 for repeated violations, effectively shutting down the football program for two years.
The 'Butkus Award' for the nation's best linebacker is named after Dick Butkus, who starred at Illinois in the 1960s.
College football introduced the two-point conversion in 1958, years before the NFL adopted it.
The 'Holy War' between BYU and Utah is one of the most heated rivalries in the Mountain West and Pac-12 regions.
Baker Mayfield won the 2017 Heisman Trophy at Oklahoma after walking on to the team and eventually becoming the starting quarterback.
The 'World's Largest Outdoor Cocktail Party' is the nickname for the Florida–Georgia rivalry game, traditionally played in Jacksonville.
The Bedlam Series between Oklahoma and Oklahoma State has been played since 1904 and is one of the most lopsided rivalries in terms of wins.
John David Crow won the 1957 Heisman Trophy at Texas A&M, the Aggies' only Heisman winner through 2024.
The 'Clock Play' in the 1994 Colorado–Michigan game saw a controversial time management decision influence the outcome.
Oklahoma's 'Boomer Sooner' fight song is played so frequently during games that opposing fans have called it the most annoying song in sports.
The 2012 SEC Championship Game between Alabama and Georgia came down to a goal-line stand, with Alabama winning 32–28.
The 'Old Oaken Bucket' is the trophy for the Indiana–Purdue rivalry, one of the oldest in the Big Ten.
George Gipp, immortalized by the phrase 'Win one for the Gipper,' played for Notre Dame in the early 1920s and died at age 25.
The 'CFP Top 25' rankings are released weekly during the season and carry significantly more weight than the AP or Coaches polls.
Alabama's 'Rammer Jammer' cheer is a postgame tradition where fans celebrate victories by chanting the opposing team's defeat.
The 'Four Horsemen' of Notre Dame—Harry Stuhldreher, Don Miller, Jim Crowley, and Elmer Layden—were immortalized by sportswriter Grantland Rice in 1924.
Travis Hunter became the first player to win both the Heisman Trophy and the Bednarik Award for best defensive player in the same season in 2024.
Conference realignment in the 2020s saw Texas and Oklahoma leave the Big 12 for the SEC, reshaping the college football landscape.
The 'Land of 10,000 Drums' at Oklahoma's Owen Field creates a unique pregame atmosphere with its massive drum line.
Jim Brown, considered one of the greatest athletes ever, starred at Syracuse before being drafted No. 6 overall by the Cleveland Browns in 1957.
The 2004 Auburn team went 13–0 but was left out of the BCS National Championship Game in favor of USC and Oklahoma.
Notre Dame's 1988 victory over No. 1 Miami, ending the Hurricanes' 36-game winning streak, is known as 'Catholics vs. Convicts.'
The split T formation, developed by Missouri's Don Faurot in the 1940s, influenced the development of modern offensive schemes.
The 'Hail Flutie' pass in 1984 helped Doug Flutie become a household name and win the Heisman Trophy.
Ohio State and Alabama have combined for the most CFP appearances among all programs since the playoff's inception.
The 'Angry Michigan Surrender Cobra' fan image became one of the most shared sports photos on social media.
Sam Bradford won the 2008 Heisman Trophy at Oklahoma and went on to be the No. 1 overall pick in the 2010 NFL Draft.
The National Championship trophy in college football has undergone several redesigns and is currently sponsored by Dr Pepper.
Georgia's mascot, Uga, is so beloved that deceased mascots are buried in a mausoleum at the southwest corner of Sanford Stadium.
The 'Bluegrass' portion of Kentucky's college football identity reflects the state's agricultural heritage.
Carson Palmer won the 2002 Heisman Trophy at USC, helping revive the program under coach Pete Carroll.
The 1946 Army-Notre Dame game, featuring Doc Blanchard and Glenn Davis against Johnny Lujack, ended in a scoreless tie and is considered one of the greatest games ever.
Red Grange, 'The Galloping Ghost,' put college football on the national map with his performance against Michigan in 1924.
The 'Miracle in Ann Arbor' in 2024 produced one of the most dramatic finishes in Michigan stadium history.
The 2015 Rose Bowl between Oregon and Florida State saw Marcus Mariota lead the Ducks to a 59–20 victory.
The 'Third Saturday in October' refers to the Tennessee–Alabama rivalry game, traditionally scheduled for that date.
NIL (Name, Image, and Likeness) legislation, which took effect in 2021, allowed college athletes to profit from their personal brands for the first time.
The 'Tombstone' at Oklahoma is a tradition where a headstone is created for each team the Sooners defeat during the season.
Eric Crouch won the 2001 Heisman Trophy at Nebraska as a dual-threat quarterback in the option offense.
The Liberty Bowl in Memphis, Tennessee, has been played since 1959 and has hosted numerous competitive matchups.
The 2024 College Football Playoff expansion to 12 teams marked the biggest structural change in the sport's postseason history.
Auburn's Jordan-Hare Stadium has a capacity of over 87,000 and is known for its intense atmosphere on game day.
The 'Bourbon Bowl' is an unofficial name sometimes given to matchups between Kentucky and Louisville.
Jason White won the 2003 Heisman Trophy at Oklahoma despite having two torn ACLs earlier in his career.
The College Football Hall of Fame moved from South Bend, Indiana, to Atlanta, Georgia, in 2014.
Texas A&M's midnight yell practice, held the night before home games, draws thousands of fans to Kyle Field.
The 'Paul Bunyan Trophy' is awarded to the winner of the Michigan–Michigan State rivalry.
DeVonta Smith made history in 2020 as the first wide receiver to win the Heisman Trophy since Desmond Howard in 1991.
Ohio State's 2002 national championship run included a dramatic overtime victory against Miami in the Fiesta Bowl with controversial pass interference calls.
The 2019 playoff semifinal saw LSU dominate Oklahoma 63–28 in the Peach Bowl en route to the national title.
Gene Stallings coached Alabama to the 1992 national championship, continuing the school's tradition of defensive excellence.
The 'Blackout' game tradition, where fans are encouraged to wear all black, originated at various schools and creates a dramatic visual effect.
The 'Legends Division' and 'Leaders Division' were short-lived Big Ten alignment names widely ridiculed by fans before being replaced.
The Davey O'Brien Award is given to the best quarterback in college football, named after TCU's 1938 Heisman winner.
Miami's 'Catholics vs. Convicts' shirt became one of the most iconic pieces of college football merchandise from the 1988 rivalry.
The 1973 'Game of the Century' between Alabama and Notre Dame in the Sugar Bowl saw Notre Dame win 24–23 to claim the national title.
Les Miles earned the nickname 'The Mad Hatter' for his unpredictable play-calling at LSU, including his habit of eating grass on the sideline.
Penn State's 'Nittanyville' tent city, where students camp out for tickets, demonstrates the passion of the Nittany Lion fanbase.
The 2020 national championship between Alabama and Ohio State was played in a mostly empty stadium due to COVID-19 restrictions.
Robert Griffin III won the 2011 Heisman Trophy at Baylor, transforming the school's football program.
Howard Schnellenberger built the Miami Hurricanes into a national powerhouse in the 1980s before the program sustained success under Jimmy Johnson and Dennis Erickson.
The 'Granddaddy of Them All' nickname for the Rose Bowl was coined by legendary announcer Keith Jackson.
Oklahoma's Selmon brothers—Lucious, Dewey, and Lee Roy—all played for the Sooners in the 1970s and all reached the NFL.
Kyle Field at Texas A&M can hold over 102,000 fans, making it one of the largest stadiums in college football.
The 'Iron Bowl' gets its name from Birmingham's history as a center of the iron and steel industry in Alabama.
Georgia's 2021 national championship team featured a historically dominant defense that held opponents to just 10.2 points per game.
The single-game rushing record in FBS history is 427 yards, set by Samaje Perine of Oklahoma in 2014.
The first College GameDay on-campus broadcast was from South Bend, Indiana, for a Notre Dame game in 1993.
Mark Ingram became the first Alabama player to win the Heisman Trophy when he claimed the award in 2009.
The 'Circle of Death' refers to the SEC West division, where multiple teams are consistently ranked in the top 25.
The 1997 split national championship between Michigan (AP) and Nebraska (Coaches) fueled the push for a definitive championship format.
TCU's unexpected run to the 2022 CFP National Championship Game under first-year coach Sonny Dykes was one of the great Cinderella stories in recent memory.
The 'Sooner Schooner' crashed at a 1993 game against Kansas, one of the more bizarre incidents in college football mascot history.
The Piesman Trophy, an informal award for linemen who score touchdowns, has become a beloved tradition on social media.
The longest field goal in college football history is 69 yards, kicked by Ove Johansson of Abilene Christian in 1976.
The 2022 Michigan team reached the CFP semifinal for the second consecutive year, signaling the program's return to elite status under Jim Harbaugh.
The 'Hook and Ladder' is one of the most exciting trick plays in college football, popularized by Boise State's Chris Petersen.
The College Football Playoff selection show has become a major television event each December, drawing millions of viewers.
USC's dominance in the early 2000s under Pete Carroll produced Heisman winners Matt Leinart and Reggie Bush in consecutive years.
The 'Fifth Quarter' at Wisconsin refers to the postgame concert by the marching band, which often draws as many fans as the game itself.
Derrick Henry won the 2015 Heisman Trophy at Alabama with a historic 2,219-yard rushing season.
The Black College Football Hall of Fame celebrates the contributions of HBCU programs and players to the sport.
The 1980 'Miracle on the Plains' featured a stunning Georgia win to cap a national championship season.
The Broyles Award is given annually to the best assistant coach in college football.
Oklahoma's 'Land Run' pre-game entrance, where the Sooner Schooner leads the team onto the field, pays tribute to the state's frontier history.
Bryce Young won the 2021 Heisman Trophy at Alabama, becoming the Crimson Tide's second Heisman winner under Nick Saban.
The 'Judgement Day' phrase is sometimes attached to rivalry games where both teams enter with national championship implications.
The 1991 'Miracle on the Farm' saw Cal beat Stanford 18–16 after Stanford missed a last-second field goal.
Caleb Williams won the 2022 Heisman Trophy at USC after transferring from Oklahoma, showcasing the impact of the transfer portal.
The 2017 Rose Bowl between USC and Penn State featured 80 combined points in a thrilling 52–49 USC victory.
Alabama's '3rd and Saban' is a fan-coined term for the coach's uncanny ability to convert crucial third-down plays.
The 2021 Michigan Wolverines broke a streak of eight consecutive losses to Ohio State with a dominant 42–27 victory.
The original 'Death Valley' nickname is claimed by both Clemson and LSU, with each school having its own origin story.
Andre Ware won the 1989 Heisman Trophy at Houston while running the run-and-shoot offense.
The 2015 Alabama–Clemson championship game was the first of four title game matchups between the two programs in a span of four years.
The Maxwell Award is given annually to the best all-around player in college football.
Southern Cal's Heritage Hall houses a collection of Heisman Trophies and other memorabilia from the program's storied history.
College football's two-minute warning does not exist; unlike the NFL, the game clock runs continuously with only standard stoppages.
The 'Shula Bowl' between Florida Atlantic and Florida International honored coach Don Shula, who grew up in the region.
The 1991 Florida State–Miami game, known as 'Wide Right I,' saw Florida State miss a last-second field goal wide right, costing them the national championship.
The concept of redshirting, where a player practices but doesn't play to preserve eligibility, is unique to college sports.
USC has won 11 national championships, tying for the fourth-most in college football history.
The 2012 Heisman ceremony was one of the most dramatic, with Johnny Manziel winning as a freshman.
The Clemson–South Carolina rivalry game has featured some of the most dramatic finishes in ACC and SEC football.
The 'Victory Lap' tradition at Notre Dame sees the marching band play the alma mater while the team celebrates with fans after a win.
The 2010 BCS National Championship Game saw Alabama defeat Texas 37–21 after Longhorns quarterback Colt McCoy was injured early.
Georgia's 'red and black' color scheme was reportedly chosen by the university's first football team in the 1890s.
Gino Torretta won the 1992 Heisman Trophy while quarterbacking Miami to a dominant regular season.
The 'Clemson Tiger Paw' was designed by a local agency in 1970 and has become one of the most recognizable logos in college sports.
The 2018 semifinal saw Clemson dismantle Notre Dame 30–3 in the Cotton Bowl, one of the most lopsided playoff games.
Tom Osborne retired after winning three national championships at Nebraska in four seasons from 1994 to 1997.
The 'Scholar-Baller' program recognizes student-athletes who maintain high academic standards while competing at the highest level.
The 1996 Fiesta Bowl, which served as the de facto national championship, featured Nebraska's 62–24 victory over Florida.
The longest punt in college football history is 99 yards, achieved by Pat Brady of Nevada in 1950.
The 'Jeweled Shillelagh' is the trophy awarded to the winner of the USC–Notre Dame rivalry, studded with rubies and emeralds.
The 1990 Colorado–Missouri 'Fifth Down' game remains one of college football's most controversial outcomes.
Minnesota's Floyd of Rosedale, a bronze pig trophy, goes to the winner of the Minnesota–Iowa rivalry each year.
The 2001 Miami Hurricanes are considered one of the most dominant teams ever, with 17 players from that roster drafted into the NFL.
The Paul Hornung Award is given to the most versatile player in college football, named after the 1956 Heisman winner from Notre Dame.
The 'Swamp' at Florida's Ben Hill Griffin Stadium is known for its oppressive heat and deafening crowd noise.
Kenneth Walker III won the 2021 Doak Walker Award at Michigan State after transferring from Wake Forest.
The longest game in college football history was a seven-overtime contest between Arkansas and Kentucky in 2003.
Notre Dame's 'Fair Catch Free Kick' rule has been rarely invoked but remains one of the most obscure rules in the rulebook.
The 'Kick Six' play in 2013 was named the greatest play in college football history by multiple media outlets.
